description: V2 oracle test facts for Q_f invariants and 3D Navier-Stokes regularity. Length-balanced distractors (ratio 0.8-1.2x) to eliminate length bias.

facts.context: The Beale-Kato-Majda (1984) criterion states that a smooth 3D Euler/Navier-Stokes solution blows up at time T if and only if:
facts.truth: ∫₀ᵀ ||ω||_{L^∞} dt = ∞ (vorticity maximum becomes infinite in finite time)
distractors: ∫₀ᵀ ||∇p||_{L²} dt = ∞ (pressure gradient norm diverges in finite time)
∫₀ᵀ ||u||_{L^∞} dt = ∞ (velocity field maximum becomes unbounded at T)
∫₀ᵀ ||∇u||_{L²} dt = ∞ (velocity gradient integrated norm blows up)

facts.context: The 3D vorticity equation ∂ω/∂t + (u·∇)ω = (ω·∇)u + ν∇²ω contains the term (ω·∇)u which is called:
facts.truth: vortex stretching (unique to 3D, absent in 2D, can amplify |ω|)
distractors: vortex advection (present in both 2D and 3D, transports |ω|)
viscous diffusion (smooths vorticity gradients, dampens |ω|)
baroclinic torque (generates vorticity from pressure gradients)

facts.context: For 3D Euler vorticity, under vortex stretching by factor s (tube stretches, thins), the standard Q_f = ∫∫ ω(x)·ω(y) f(|x-y|) d³x d³y transforms as:
facts.truth: quadratic growth in s (Q_f scales as s² under axial stretching)
distractors: linear growth in s (Q_f scales proportionally to the stretch factor)
logarithmic growth in s (Q_f scales as ln(s) under axial stretching)
inverse decay in s (Q_f scales as 1/s under the stretching deformation)

facts.context: In 3D Euler, the kinetic energy E = (1/2)∫|u|²dx corresponds to Q_{1/r}. Under vortex stretching, energy is:
facts.truth: exactly conserved (stretching redistributes but cannot create energy)
distractors: quadratically amplified (stretching generates energy proportionally)
slowly dissipated (stretching gradually converts energy to entropy)
approximately conserved (small losses accumulate during stretching)

facts.context: The ratio R_f = Q_{e^(-r)} / Q_{1/r} for 3D vortex dynamics under stretching is:
facts.truth: more stable than either Q_f alone (both scale as s², ratio partially cancels)
distractors: more sensitive than either Q_f alone (ratio amplifies s² scaling further)
completely independent of the stretching factor s (ratio is exactly one)
undefined when either Q_f diverges under large stretching deformations

facts.context: Numerical tests of R_f = Q_{exp} / Q_{inv} on 3D vortex ring evolution show stretch resistance of:
facts.truth: 2% variation under stretching (vs 60% for standard Q_f)
distractors: 60% variation under stretching (comparable to standard Q_f)
0.001% variation under stretching (machine precision exact)
35% variation under stretching (moderate improvement over Q_f)

facts.context: For 3D Euler, the enstrophy-normalized Q_f / Ω (where Ω = ∫|ω|² dx) behaves under stretching as:
facts.truth: 17% variation (better than 60% for unnormalized, worse than R_f at 2%)
distractors: 0.1% variation (nearly exact conservation, outperforming all R_f ratios)
60% variation (enstrophy normalization provides no improvement at all)
45% variation (marginal improvement over unnormalized standard form)

facts.context: The curvature-weighted Q_f^κ = ∫∫ κ(x)κ(y) ω·ω f(r) d³x d³y shows stretch resistance of:
facts.truth: 4% variation (emphasizes bent regions, reduces when tubes straighten)
distractors: 50% variation (curvature weighting destabilizes the invariant further)
0.01% variation (curvature weighting produces near-exact conservation)
25% variation (comparable performance to enstrophy normalization)

facts.context: For 2D Navier-Stokes with viscosity ν, the Q_f quantities decay as:
facts.truth: dQ_f/dt ∝ -ν (linear in viscosity), recovering Euler conservation as ν→0
distractors: dQ_f/dt ∝ -ν² (quadratic in viscosity), vanishing faster than linear decay
dQ_f/dt ∝ -ν^(3/2) (fractional power), intermediate between linear/quadratic
dQ_f/dt ∝ -√ν (square root of viscosity), decaying slower than linear rate

facts.context: For a Gaussian vortex with width σ and circulation Γ, the relationship between Q_r and ||ω||_∞ is:
distractors: ||ω||_∞ ∝ Q_r² (vorticity peak grows quadratically with Q_r)
||ω||_∞ ∝ exp(Q_r) (vorticity peak grows exponentially with Q_r)
||ω||_∞ ∝ Q_r^(1/2) (vorticity peak grows as square root of Q_r)

facts.context: For 2D Euler/Navier-Stokes, Q_f conservation combined with BKM implies:
facts.truth: no finite-time blowup (consistent with known 2D global regularity)
distractors: finite-time blowup is possible (Q_f cannot prevent 2D singularities)
only local-in-time existence (Q_f bounds are too weak to extend globally)
conditional regularity only (Q_f requires additional smallness assumptions)

facts.context: The main obstacle to using Q_f for 3D NS regularity is:
facts.truth: vortex stretching breaks Q_f conservation (except for energy Q_{1/r})
distractors: viscous dissipation breaks Q_f conservation (except for enstrophy Q_r²)
pressure terms break Q_f conservation (except for helicity Q_{curl})
boundary conditions break Q_f conservation (except for momentum Q_lin)

facts.context: Physically, the ratio R_f = Q_{e^(-r)} / Q_{1/r} measures:
facts.truth: locality of vorticity interactions (how much dynamics depends on nearby vs distant vorticity)
distractors: rate of enstrophy dissipation (how quickly vorticity variance decays over time)
helicity density distribution (how vortex tube linking varies across scales)
energy cascade direction (whether energy transfers to larger or smaller scales)

facts.context: In 3D ideal fluids, the helicity H = ∫ u·ω dx is:
facts.truth: exactly conserved (topological invariant of vortex line linking)
distractors: approximately conserved (slowly decays due to nonlinear interactions)
not conserved in general (only preserved for axisymmetric geometries)
monotonically increasing (grows under vortex stretching amplification)

facts.context: The Clay Millennium Problem for 3D Navier-Stokes asks whether:
facts.truth: smooth solutions remain smooth for all time, or if singularities can develop
distractors: weak solutions are unique given arbitrary square-integrable initial velocity data
turbulent flows converge to statistical equilibrium in the infinite time limit
viscous solutions approach inviscid Euler solutions as viscosity tends to zero

facts.context: The combined score (stretch + evolution conservation) for R_f = Q_{exp}/Q_{inv} is:
facts.truth: 0.59% (best among all tested variants including enstrophy-norm and curvature-weighted)
distractors: 2.44% (second-best after curvature-weighted Q_f which achieves 0.31% combined)
6.40% (third-best, outperformed by both enstrophy-norm and standard variants)
12.7% (worst among tested variants, standard Q_f outperforms at 2.95% combined)
